text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.
I’m checking the repo layout and the current Next.js cache API so I can wire the server action to invalidate the right tag without serving stale content.

I confirmed the Next.js behavior: `updateTag('posts')` is the right API for a server action when you need the next request to wait for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content. Next I’m locating the posts action and cache tag wiring in the app.

text: The app is minimal, so I’m reading the page and package files to see how to add the action cleanly and whether there’s already a data layer to hook into.

I have enough context to implement this cleanly: a cached posts reader tagged with `posts`, a server action that mutates the backing store, and `updateTag('posts')` so the next request blocks on fresh data instead of serving stale cache. I’m adding the smallest working example around the existing page.

I’m replacing the placeholder page with a tagged cached data source and a real server action. The key change is `updateTag('posts')` after the mutation, with the posts reader actually tagged so the invalidation has an effect.
I’ve got the data source and invalidation in place, but I need to fix one naming collision in the page so the server action compiles cleanly. Then I’ll sanity-check the final shape.
I found a compile issue: the page imports `createPost` and also declares a server action with the same name. I’m renaming the local action and keeping the cache invalidation behavior unchanged.

The implementation is now aligned with the requirement: the post list is cached under `posts`, and the server action calls `updateTag('posts')` after mutation so the next load waits for fresh data instead of serving stale cache. I’m running a build check to catch any remaining Next/TypeScript issues.
The build is still running. Once it finishes, I’ll confirm whether the new server action and cache invalidation type-check cleanly or if there’s one more adjustment needed.

text:
Implemented.

- Added a server action that creates a post and invalidates the `posts` cache tag with `updateTag('posts')`, which is the Next.js API that guarantees the next request waits for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content.
- Added a tagged cached post reader so the invalidation actually applies to the posts list.
- Verified with `npm run build`; the app compiles successfully.

Files:
- [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx)
- [app/posts.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/posts.ts)

Docs used:
- [Next.js `updateTag`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag)
- [Next.js `revalidateTag`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag)

If you want, I can also wire this to a real database instead of the in-memory example.
